Caption: Sketch of the pouch cell and loading cases: (a) top view and (b) dimetric view, where the black dots indicate the indentation positions. (c) out-of-plane hemispherical (d) cylindrical indentations and (e) in-plane hemispherical indentations.

Lithium-ion pouch cell materials. (a) Cross-sectional view of the jelly stack with ① the pouch cell, ② the anode with the copper current collector and ③ the anode current collector. Scanning Electron Micrographs of jelly stack materials. (b) separator with submicron-sized porosities. (c) active material and (d) graphite.
Punches used for the out-of-plane indentation. From left to right R = 2.5 , 5 , 10 , 15 , 25 , 50 m m . The smallest punch is also used for in-plane indentation.
Calibrated two-stage hardening law.
Undeformed meshes for (a) out-of-plane and (b) in-plane indentation.
Experimental results for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with increasing radii from (a) R = 2.5 m m to (f) R = 50 m m .
Position dependency of the force–displacement response for the out-of-plane indentation with radius (a) R = 2.5 m m and (b) R = 5 m m .
(a) Normalized force–displacement response under out-of-plane hemispherical indentation. Evolution of (b) the maximum force and (c) the critical displacement as a function of the indenter radius.

Deformed views under out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with a radius R = 10 m m . (a) to (d) vertical displacement of the cell obtained from 3D-DIC and (e) deformed profile of the bottom side of the cell.
Evolution of the top surface of the cell: (a) vertical displacement, (b) flatness and (c) angle to indenter axis.
Out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with an indenter radius of (a) 5 mm, (b) 10 mm and (c) 15 mm. (d) Normalized force–displacement curves for all three radii.

Deformation of the cell under cylindrical out-of-plane indentation. (a)-(d) Deformed cells at increased displacement with superimposed vertical displacement profile. (e) Corresponding mid-section vertical displacement. The cyan cross delimits the region used for linear fitting (represented as cyan dashed lines). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
In-plane hemispherical indentation. The out-of-plane indentation (black line) is shown for comparison purposes.

Selected micrographs of the out-of-plane and in-plane hemispherical indentations. (a) to (f) out-of-plane indentations with radii R = 2.5 , 5 , 10 , 15 , 25 , 50 m m and in-plane indentation along the (g) long and (h) short cell side.
Numerical results for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with increasing radii from (a) R = 2.5 m m to (f) R = 50 m m .
Numerical results for the out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with increasing radii from (a) R = 5 m m to (f) R = 15 m m .
Numerical results for both in-plane hemispherical indentations.
Deformed meshes for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with increasing radii from (a) R = 2.5 m m to (f) R = 50 m m . The colormap represents the equivalent stress.

Deformed meshes for the out-of-plane hemispherical indentation with radius R = 10 m m at various displacement level (a,c,e) and for out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with radius R = 10 m m . The displacement level corresponds to Fig. 6c and Fig. 11b respectively. The colormap represents the equivalent stress.
Deformed meshes for the out-of-plane cylindrical indentation with increasing radii from (a) R = 5 m m to (f) R = 15 m m . The colormap represents the equivalent stress.
Deformed meshes for the in-plane hemispherical indentations along both sides of the cells at a displacement of 4.2 mm. The colormap represents the equivalent stress.

Evolution of the strain state at the top of the cell right under the punch and at the half thickness. (a)-(b) Triaxiality for the hemispherical and cylindrical out-of-plane indentation and (c)-(d) Lode angle parameter.
